it's great we're getting started on this. we have a great opportunity here to design an API that lasts and is nice to use :-)  i have a few comments and questions, happy to discuss.
 
well done on the performance measurement and caching! do we have an idea of how many clients are cached after, for example, the integration test suite has run through?

## external vs internal clients

i'm still a bit confused external and internal clients. i understand that we need:

* a client that talks to ASF services
* a client that talks to some other AWS compatible backend (like proxying to dynamodb). so basically a regular botocore client.

but why does the say "Build and return client for connections **originating** outside LocalStack". Aren't we connect from localstack to an "external" service?

sorry for the stupid question. can you briefly outline the difference and what they are used for? i feel we could use this as an opportunity to create more clarity for other developers as well.

## connect to

from localstack.aws.connect import connect_to
```python
connect_to('sns', source_service='s3', target_arn='arn:aws:sns:ap-south-1:000000000000:lorem')\
.publish(TopicArn='arn:aws:sns:ap-south-1:000000000000:lorem', message='blah')
```

just thinking out loud: does the `target_arn` always appear somewhere in the request? could we generalize and hide this from the user by defining a set of declarations? e.g., `target_arn_selector[("sns", "TopicArn")] = "TopicArn"`? or is this too inflexible/complicated/hard to maintain?

in that case, there would also be a way to generalize the `connect_to` signature, which would give us a way to generalize the client factory interface.
so instead of having a `ConnectFactory` with `get_internal_client` and `get_external_client`, we'd have a `ASFClientFactory` and an `BotoClientFactory`. that would be nicer from a design perspective, but i could imagine this is maybe not possible or practical?

It appears this will generally call latest versions, even if say case default is 2.0 and institute latest is 3.0? While it is great to have the option to filter with 3.0, I'd say the default behaviour should still be filter with 2.0 unless explicitly chosen otherwise?

The default behavior in current master is that if even if a case has an associated panel of version 2.0, then the filter filters by the latest panel. This is because the query is looking for variants having the key/value:

This PR is not changing the result, just the query: search for genes instead of panels.

I think a marginally nicer way to eliminate the global `LIBRARIES` variable would be to have a class which takes `libraries` as a parameter and stores it as an attribute, then make all the functions that now take a `libraries` parameter methods of that class instead. But only marginally nicer. Honestly, if I were that allergic to OOP-destroying global variables then I would have abandoned Python altogether in favour pedantically object oriented, but otherwise underwhelming at every turn, Java :upside_down_face: .

For pytorch, since `model.train(train: bool)` is ubiquitous across models, I think it makes more sense to migrate `set_learning_phase` from `PyTorchClassifier` to `PyTorchEstimator`, as the former inherits from the latter. This would also make it easier to add new PyTorch models like a trainable ASR model without rewriting that method for each. Similarly, if users create their own PyTorch estimators and wrap them with PyTorchEstimator, they would need to reimplement that.

For similar reasons, I would recommend moving the TensorFlow functions to the estimator class. (I understand that you may want to override that for the classifier class, due to the use of the feed_dict for V1, but I think that will make it easier to subclass from estimator.)

I think a similar approach could apply to mxnet, keras, and scikitlearn estimators.

Thanks for taking care of this!

> I chose to use the PyPi URL for the download of the dependency as I had difficulties finding the sha256 hashes for the version of the library at mirror.tensorflow.org and github.com (they differ from the PyPi one).

It's fine to just generate the sha256 hash locally - i.e. download the tarball and compute the hash yourself.  In this case it might be easier to still do that, since the github one is already mirrored (see other comment).

FWIW, to justify that a little - the point of the hash isn't really to verify the integrity of the file that you're downloading today from the server, because in general we assume you're downloading it via HTTPS, on a reasonably secure machine and network, etc, and it doesn't really add much protection to separately get a hash from the same server rather than just computing it yourself.  The point is to ensure the server doesn't change the contents of the file in the future.  That way, we reduce the amount of trust we need to have from "I trust this server in perpetuity" to "I trust this server right now, today".

Thanks a lot @beat-buesser for addressing all the comments. This looks good to me.

> Is there a reason why we are preferring Werkzeug over SimpleHTTPServer?

So, the Python stdlib has two related things. First is `http.server`,
which was called `SimpleHTTPServer` in Python 2. This is a static server
that only serves from disk. Great for lots of cases, but not useful here
since we need to serve the contents of a zip file. We could extract it
and serve from disk, but then you have to re-extract each time the
contents change, so you have to clean up a tempdir or keep some state
somewhere. That sounds more complicated than this.

The other is `wsgiref`, which provides “a simple HTTP server” that is
not the same as `SimpleHTTPServer`: namely, `wsgiref.simple_server`. We
could definitely use this, and I did so in bazelbuild/rules_rust#475 to
avoid pulling in a Werkzeug dep. But we already have Werkzeug, so I
thought that this was just a bit simpler.

At least for me, our `@org_pocoo_werkzeug` dep doesn’t actually seem to
be linking right, in that when I run this server outside of a virtualenv
it can’t find `werkzeug`. I judged this fine since we all obviously use
TensorBoard virtualenvs, anyway. But it is a reasonable point in favor
of just using the standard library.

Would you prefer that I avoid Werkzeug for the app and the server? You
can see what that’d look like here:
<https://github.com/bazelbuild/rules_rust/pull/475/files#diff-806deb54546ccb18b46d212494d862925348c2e9d939c9e88cbf3b79e81ec4a1R1>

> I really would like to understand how ibazel support works. […] Does
> saving a rust file restart the server or something?

Yep. There’s a transitive build dep:

```
$ bazel query 'somepath(:rustboard_core_doc_server, :lib.rs)'
//tensorboard/data/server:rustboard_core_doc_server
//tensorboard/data/server:rustboard_core_doc.zip
//tensorboard/data/server:rustboard_core_doc
//tensorboard/data/server:rustboard_core
//tensorboard/data/server:lib.rs
```

@alexrashed @thrau the outstanding comments seem to be mostly talking about the API design of `HostAndPort`. I've made some simplifications, namely:
* the default fallbacks are now required, meaning the caller is responsible for knowing what the defaults should be which makes sense. @thrau suggested this class might be useful outside of `GATEWAY_LISTEN` and this change helps that.
* `port` is no longer optional, since we always end up with one after the changes to the `parse` method, and the class _is_ named like it always has a port
* I've switched from a dataclass to regular class since there is a bit of behaviour, and I've overridden a few of the default provided methods anyway. I've added a `__repr__` method to replace the missing functionality that `dataclass` provided
* I've added some documentation since that was 100% missing

I've also removed the hypothesis test and package since it doesn't (yet) create a ROI for its inclusion, though I'm itching to use it - we do a _lot_ of parsing... :joy: I've replaced the property testing with individual tests covering the functionality.

Thanks both for this detailed feedback

> To be consistent with the renaming of "resource_defs" to "resources" not including defs suffix

My understanding of the reason we went with "resources" instead of "resource_defs" was that we actually aren't requiring `ResourceDefinition` objects as the values. Is the idea that we would do that here as well? Do we want to make a general shift in our public APIs away from the `_def` suffix?

> Not calling it "default". This implies that in the definitions world the common case will be using this API rather than specifying it on the job-level.

I don't have a strong opinion here in either direction, but wanted to call out that an advantage of `default_executor` over `executor` is that it makes it super clear what takes precedence if an executor is supplied both on the `Definitions` and on a job.

> Marking experimental to allow for change when we make resources late-binding on jobs as well to provide option value on this API.

What would we say to users who are using these arguments on `@repository` and want to stick with stable, non-deprecated APIs?

Taking a look at this, if gitpython is going to disallow passing the `--upload-pack/--receive-pack` and friends as options, the check should probably be in all other commands where those options are allowed (and should also check for their short form `-u`).

And reading from https://git-scm.com/docs/gitremote-helpers

> A URL of the form `<transport>::<address>` explicitly instructs Git to invoke `git remote-<transport>` with `<address>` as the second argument. If such a URL is encountered directly on the command line, the first argument is `<address>`, and if it is encountered in a configured remote, the first argument is the name of that remote.

Maybe is safer to disallow `foo::bar` like URLs,

Maybe using a [`Timer`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#timer-objects) would make things cleaner.

- Every handler execution starts a new timer (unless we don't have enough "buffer"/threshold).
- Whenever the original handler finishes running (the new `finally` block in this PR) and a timer has been started, call `timer.cancel()` -- no additional condition.
- The Timer runs a function that raises the timeout exception, no conditions.

The above is my suggestion.

---

**Side note**
Because the runtime can abort/pause the handler at any time, I imagine there'd be room for the timer firing incorrectly into a future handler execution, but not sure if there's anything we could do about it. (I thought of an Event to mark the end of the handler and the timer could check it, but that doesn't change anything if the `finally` block of one execution runs into the next execution -- depends how the timeout is implemented by the runtime.)
I'm going to assume that WQ support this functionality and that this was already tested. The changes look reasonable.

As I understand you haven't found the pg_basebackup output in the Patroni log. How logs are configured? Do you use a rotating handler (Patroni itself writes logs to files in a specific directory)? If yes, then in this specific case the output is not lost, it just didn't get into the Patroni log. You probably should also capture stdout and stderr of Patroni, pg_basebackup will write to the same streams and therefore you will see its output.

When the database size is a few TB it could take hours for pg_basebackup to finish.
Patroni allows configuring custom options for pg_basebackup, like for example `--progress`. If we start capturing stdout and stderr, it will become impossible to follow the basebackup progress in logs.

This approach won't work, unfortunately.

The `actions/upload-artifact` action uploads an [artifact](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/advanced-guides/storing-workflow-data-as-artifacts), rather than a "release asset" (I misspoke and called it a "release artifact" in our slack thread). 

I suggest that we:
1. Create a workflow that  is triggered by the [release](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/learn-github-actions/events-that-trigger-workflows#webhook-events) event:
  ```yaml
on:
    release:
      - published
 ```
2. This workflow would then upload the json schema using one of the following actions:
  a. The unmaintained [actions/upload-release-asset](https://github.com/actions/upload-release-asset) action (👍🏾: easy, 👎🏾 : needs fork, 3PP, and SA).
  b. The third party [alexellis/upload-assets](https://github.com/alexellis/upload-assets) action (👍🏾: easy, 👎🏾 : needs fork, 3PP, and SA).
  c. Using [SFDO-Tooling/github-script](https://github.com/SFDO-Tooling/github-script) to create a release asset via [Ocktokit](https://octokit.github.io/rest.js/v18#repos-upload-release-asset) ➡️ [REST API](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/reference/repos#upload-a-release-asset)  (👍🏾: no fork/3PP, 👎🏾: code) 

Since this is a non-trivial amount of work I think it's okay to link directly to `main` if we want to go ahead and submit the schema to schema store while we figure out our approach.

assert ak.any(ak.Array(["one", "two", "three"]) != ak.Array(["one", "two", "3"]))

For my understanding, in the flow from the network through Plasma to building the DataFrame, where was the large memory inflation happening? My understanding of the flow is:
1. Plasma buffers are allocated and filled as objects are pulled, with the Arrow tables being deserialized directly into the Plasma buffers.
2. The block builder holds pointers to those Plasma buffers, so no new copies are made while add blocks to the builder.
3. The `.build()` and `.to_pandas()` calls create a copy or two during the Arrow table concatenation and the DataFrame construction, respectively.

This PR deals with stages (1) and (2) rather than the copy-heavy stage (3), and AFAICT wouldn't result in a lower peak during those stages. Am I missing the creation of an extra copy or two in (1) and (2), maybe receiver-side queueing of byte chunks during pulls, or something else?
